{{COURSE_CODE}} / {{COURSE_NAME}}
Materials Hands-on FAQ Setup
Getting Started

Setup, once.

Everything you need to study {{COURSE_CODE}} with an AI tutor: which models to pick, how to feed them the textbook, and the prompting habits that pay off across the term.

In this guide
  1. 01 Getting course materials
  2. 02 Using DeepSeek
  3. 03 Cursor IDE (optional)
  4. 04 Choosing a model
  5. 05 Habits that pay off
§ 01

Course materials

Textbook PDF chapters are shared through Zulip in our class channel. Download the relevant chapter before each week's study session.

Textbook

{{TEXTBOOK_INFO}}

  1. Join the Zulip class channel

    Check your email or Canvas for the Zulip invitation link, then join the course channel.

  2. Download chapter PDFs

    Find the pinned messages or weekly announcement and download the chapter for the upcoming week.

  3. Open the weekly learning sheet

    Visit the materials page to access this week's sheet, prompts, and validation set.

§ 02

Studying with DeepSeek

DeepSeek is free, accepts PDF uploads, and is strong at step-by-step reasoning. If you don't already have a preferred model, start here.

Why DeepSeek
  • Free — no subscription required
  • PDF upload — feed it the chapter directly
  • Long context — whole chapters fit in one window
  • Deep Think — slower, careful reasoning mode
  1. Create an account

    Go to chat.deepseek.com and sign up with email or Google.

  2. Upload the chapter PDF

    Use the 📎 attachment button. Wait for the file to finish processing before you start asking questions.

  3. Turn on Deep Think

    For proofs and tricky steps, enable Deep Think mode. The output is slower but the reasoning is much sharper.

  4. Run the prompts

    Copy a #prompt block from the learning sheet, paste it into DeepSeek, and start. New chat per chapter.

Two prompts to keep handy

Based on the chapter I uploaded, explain:
1. The main concept introduced in §N.K
2. A worked example, step by step
3. The single most common mistake students make.
I'm confused about [topic]. Can you:
1. Explain the intuition in plain English first
2. Show me a small example
3. Then give me a practice problem similar to,
   but not identical to, the example.
Pro tip

Always start a new chat when you switch chapters. Mixed-context sessions hallucinate more, especially on definitions.

§ 03

Cursor IDE (optional)

For students who already live in a code editor, Cursor integrates AI directly. Useful for proof-heavy work where you'd like to drop Typst or Markdown alongside your notes.

  1. Install

    Get Cursor from cursor.com — Windows, macOS, and Linux all supported.

  2. Network configuration (China)

    If you're on the mainland and need access to Anthropic / OpenAI models:

    • Cursor Settings → search "HTTP" → set HTTP Compatibility mode to HTTP 1.1
    • Open Settings (Cmd/Ctrl + ,) → search "proxy" → configure your proxy
  3. Switch to Agent mode

    In the AI pane (bottom-left of chat), select Agent. Multi-step reasoning works much better than the default chat mode.

§ 04

Picking a model

Different models have different strengths. A short field guide for the 2026 landscape:

DeepSeek Free
Strong default. Free, accepts PDFs, careful reasoning in Deep Think mode.
Claude 4.7
Best for explanation and proof checking. The most patient tutor in the lineup.
Gemini 3 Pro
Long-context champion. Whole textbooks in one window, PhD-level reasoning.
GPT-5.1
Balanced all-rounder. Good fallback when other models hit rate limits.
Recommendation

Start with DeepSeek. If you want a second opinion on a hard proof, paste the same prompt into Claude. Triangulating two models catches more mistakes than trusting one.

§ 05

Habits that pay off

Ask better questions

  • Be specific. "Explain step 3 of this argument" beats "explain this."
  • Demand examples. Concrete examples solidify abstract concepts faster than rephrasing.
  • Try first. Attempt the problem before asking AI for the answer — you'll know what's actually confusing.
  • Verify by quizzing. Ask the AI to test you, not just to explain. Recall is the real check.

Study together, with different models

In study groups, agree to use different AI tools on the same problem. Compare answers. Disagreements between models almost always point at the spot where the textbook is itself ambiguous.

The 5W2H framework

Force AI responses to give you more than a paraphrase by asking the seven questions:

  • What — what is concept X?
  • Why — why does the property hold?
  • When — when do I apply technique A vs B?
  • Where — where in the proof do we use assumption Y?
  • Who — who developed this idea, and what motivated it?
  • How — how do I convert representation A into representation B?
  • How much — how sensitive is the result to parameter X?
Remember

The goal isn't to outsource thinking. AI is a patient tutor that helps you understand. The closed-book test next week measures what you can do.

{{COURSE_CODE}} · {{INSTITUTION}} Built with Typst, scaffolded by TDAA-Go.
